claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right
circumstances: Child is 18 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions.

Let me start with the first one:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm inquiring about my son Alex, who's 18 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I understand you're inquiring about your son Alex, who is 18 years old.

Since Alex is over 16, his eligibility for Child Benefit depends on whether he's in approved education or training. 

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (This typically includes things like A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3, or similar qualifications)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is. Alex is studying for his A-levels at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Since Alex is studying for his A-levels, he could potentially qualify for Child Benefit as he's under 20 and in approved education.

Now I need to ask about your living situation:

**Do you live in the UK, and does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester. And yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Now I need to understand a bit more about Alex's current situation to ensure he still qualifies:

**Has Alex started any paid work for 24 hours or more per week, or is he receiving any benefits in his own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He doesn't work that many hours - just a small part-time job. But yes, he does receive Universal Credit in his own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. This is a key detail for Alex's eligibility.

According to the Child Benefit rules, you stop being eligible for Child Benefit if your child starts getting certain benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit, even if they're still in approved education.

Let me confirm one final detail: **When did Alex start receiving Universal Credit in his own name?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He started receiving it a few months ago - around the time he turned 18 and needed some financial support.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: Although Alex is 18, living with you in the UK, and studying for his A-levels (which is approved education), the rules state that Child Benefit stops when a child starts receiving certain benefits in their own right, including Universal Credit.

Since Alex has been receiving Universal Credit in his own name since around the time he turned 18, your Child Benefit eligibility for him would have ended at that point, regardless of the fact that he's still in education.

If Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if he stops receiving Universal Credit and remains in approved education or training), you may want to check your eligibility again at that time.

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and is studying for his A-levels, which is approved education. He lives with his parent in the UK (Manchester). Normally, children under 20 in approved education would qualify for Child Benefit. However, according to the rules, Child Benefit eligibility ends if a child starts getting certain benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit. Since Alex has been receiving Universal Credit in his own name for a few months (since around when he turned 18), the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, regardless of his education status.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (18), who lives with them in the UK and is studying for A-levels. While Alex would otherwise qualify based on age and education status, he is receiving Universal Credit in his own right, which disqualifies the claimant from receiving Child Benefit for him under the rules that state Child Benefit stops when a child starts getting certain benefits such as Universal Credit.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 18 years old and is studying for his A-levels, which is approved education. He lives with his parent in the UK (Manchester). Normally, children under 20 in approved education would qualify for Child Benefit. However, according to the rules, Child Benefit eligibility ends if a child starts getting certain benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit. Since Alex has been receiving Universal Credit in his own name for a few months (since around when he turned 18), the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, regardless of his education status.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (18), who lives with them in the UK and is studying for A-levels. While Alex would otherwise qualify based on age and education status, he is receiving Universal Credit in his own right, which disqualifies the claimant from receiving Child Benefit for him under the rules that state Child Benefit stops when a child starts getting certain benefits such as Universal Credit.
